coredata.dc:title: Visible-light-switched radical/non-radical bichannel degradation mechanism via round-the-clock synergistic CDs-BiO1-xCl/PS system

coredata.dc:description: In order to achieve efficient degradation of organic pollutants (OPs) and to avoid interference from co-existing anions and natural organic matter, a synergistic CDs-BiO1-xCl/PS system is designed, which is capable of functioning under both dark and visible-light-controlled conditions, enabling round-the-clock operation. A novel nano photocatalyst CDs-BiO1-xCl was constructed by modifying the surface oxygen vacancy (Ov) rich bismuth oxychloride (BiO1-xCl) with carbon quantum dots (CDs). The key role of the edge carbon atoms (C) of CDs in CDs-BiO1-xCl photocatalyst for PS (persulfate) activation was revealed. Electron paramagnetic resonance spectroscopy, In-situ Raman, quenching experiments and density functional theory (DFT) analysis indicated that the C-edge atoms in the photocatalyst acted as an electron donor which facilitated the complexes formation with PS under dark conditions, and the complexes participated in organic pollutants degradation via electron transfer (non-free radical) pathways subsequently. The system cleverly utilizes the “on-off” of light to achieve the controlled triggering of free radical and non-free radical pathways for PS activation. Upon light irradiation, photogenerated carriers migrate toward CDs, promoting further decomposition of its surface complexes to produce sulfate radicals (·SO4 -). Hence, this optimized light-controlled synergistic system showed complete removal of BPA (10 mg/L) in the presence of 2 mM PS within 15 min via free radical and non-free radical pathways. The visible-light-driven system did not produce any toxic byproducts and showed excellent stability under various reaction conditions. Therefore, the round-the-clock and photo-switching-regulated high-efficiency CDs-BiO1-xCl/PS system demonstrates promising application prospects for removing organic pollutants in complex water bodies.

head.ce:title: Visible-light-switched radical/non-radical bichannel degradation mechanism via round-the-clock synergistic CDs-BiO1-xCl/PS system

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In order to achieve efficient degradation of organic pollutants (OPs) and to avoid interference from co-existing anions and natural organic matter, a synergistic CDs-BiO1-xCl/PS system is designed, which is capable of functioning under both dark and visible-light-controlled conditions, enabling round-the-clock operation. A novel nano photocatalyst CDs-BiO1-xCl was constructed by modifying the surface oxygen vacancy (Ov) rich bismuth oxychloride (BiO1-xCl) with carbon quantum dots (CDs). The key role of the edge carbon atoms (C) of CDs in CDs-BiO1-xCl photocatalyst for PS (persulfate) activation was revealed. Electron paramagnetic resonance spectroscopy, In-situ Raman, quenching experiments and density functional theory (DFT) analysis indicated that the C-edge atoms in the photocatalyst acted as an electron donor which facilitated the complexes formation with PS under dark conditions, and the complexes participated in organic pollutants degradation via electron transfer (non-free radical) pathways subsequently. The system cleverly utilizes the “on-off” of light to achieve the controlled triggering of free radical and non-free radical pathways for PS activation. Upon light irradiation, photogenerated carriers migrate toward CDs, promoting further decomposition of its surface complexes to produce sulfate radicals (·SO4 -). Hence, this optimized light-controlled synergistic system showed complete removal of BPA (10 mg/L) in the presence of 2 mM PS within 15 min via free radical and non-free radical pathways. The visible-light-driven system did not produce any toxic byproducts and showed excellent stability under various reaction conditions. Therefore, the round-the-clock and photo-switching-regulated high-efficiency CDs-BiO1-xCl/PS system demonstrates promising application prospects for removing organic pollutants in complex water bodies.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Among various remediation strategies for refractory pollutants in different water bodies that have harmful impacts on the ecosystems and human health, the advanced oxidation processes (AOP) have been regarded as a future-proof and effective strategy. In recent years, concerns have escalated regarding the presence of organic pollutants (OPs) such as bisphenol A (BPA), acetaminophen (AAP), phenol, and rhodamine B (RhB) in various environmental matrices, highlighting potential risks to both human health and ecological systems. Advanced oxidation processes (AOPs) can produce sufficient reactive oxygen species (ROSs) (e.g., hydroxyl radicals (·OH), sulfate radicals (·SO4 -) and superoxide radical (·O2 -)), which decompose the refractory pollutants into less harmful products (Chen et al., 2021). Compared with traditional AOPs, visible light photocatalysis is an environmentally friendly technology that can be used to transform solar energy into chemical energy. Although photocatalysis has many advantages, the limitations of conventional photocatalysis in the practical treatment of refractory pollutants are obvious. On the one hand, co-existing anions and natural organic matters (NOMs) in the actual water react with these highly reactive radicals, inhibiting the degradation of the pollutants and also potentially generating secondary pollutants with higher toxicity. On the other hand, continuous light irradiation is a prerequisite for driving the photocatalytic redox reaction, which severely hinders the widespread use of photocatalytic redox reactions, especially on rainy and dark days. Once the external light irradiation stops, the photo-induced carriers are no longer produced in the semiconductor photocatalysts, resulting in an immediate loss of catalytic ability. Therefore, exploring effective strategies to realize that the visible-light-modulated radical/non-radical complementary degradation pathways inhibiting the interference of co-existing anions and NOMs to achieve the round-the-clock degradation of pollutants is crucial for the practical remediation of organic pollution in water bodies.

It is well noticed that the coupled photocatalysis-persulfate activated (CPPA) system is an effective strategy to achieve visible-light-regulated round-the-clock pollutant degradation. Recently, this system gained much attention due to its cooperative effect between persulfate (PS) activation and photocatalysis as it plays a crucial role in efficient photodegradation of organic contaminants. In such a system, PS acts as an acceptor for photoinduced electrons generating ·SO4 - and other ROSs, which result in enhanced interfacial charge separation and therefore optimized photocatalytic activity (Qiu et al., 2021). Thus, exploring catalysts with efficient visible light harvesting capacity and low recombination rate of photogenerated charge carriers are the key issues of the system. In addition, considering the possible chemical activation in CPPA system, the system can continuously activate PS to produce oxidative complexes to complementally degrade pollutants in the absence of light, resulting in round-the-clock pollutant degradation. Thus, exploring catalysts with highly efficient photocatalytic and chemical co-activation properties are the key point in the development of the CPPA system. Furthermore, it is promising to liberally regulate the radical and non-radical activation pathways of PS by synergizing the surface modification of the catalysts and the adjustment of the carrier transfer via "ON-OFF" of light in complex aqueous environments (Wang et al., 2022). However, the role of non-metal materials in radical and non-radical transformation under light switching has barely been investigated in the round-the-clock CPPA system.

In general, the proper construction and synthesis of catalysts for efficient visible light trapping and chemical activation of PS is the key to establish a round-the-clock light-controlled CPPA system for the remediation of polluted water. Recently, bismuth oxychloride (BiOCl), as a novel low toxicity, low-cost layered semiconductor photocatalyst, showed the excellent photocatalytic oxidation and reduction ability due to that the typical layered structure of BiOCl facilitates the migration of photoinduced charge carriers. Furthermore, [Bi2O2]2+ and Cl- components in BiOCl form a built-in electric field, which can inhibit the charge recombination of photoinduced e- -h+ pairs (Gu et al., 2019). In addition, oxygen vacancies (Ov) as defects in BiOCl (BiO1-xCl) extend the light response range and provide a convenient way to accelerate the charge separation for improved photocatalytic activity (Wu et al., 2017). Although BiO1-xCl has excellent photocatalytic potential, it cannot chemically activate PS to degrade organic pollutants. Therefore, BiO1-xCl alone is not sufficient to build a round-the-clock light-controlled CPPA system. Surprisingly, the abundant surface functional groups of carbon quantum dots (CDs) can provide plenty of reaction sites for PS complexation. The carbon atoms at the edges of carbon materials (C) showed the formation of oxidizing complexes with PS that participate in pollutant degradation (Ding et al., 2021). In addition, CDs have attracted great research interest because of their low biotoxicity, distinctive photo-induced electron transfer capacity, and photon conversion properties (Qi et al., 2022). It has been determined that the introduction of CDs significantly increases the reaction kinetics during photocatalysis owing to their high light-harvesting efficiency, charge conductivity, and improved catalytic active sites for redox reactions (Zhang et al., 2021). Considering these outstanding features of oxygen-deficient BiOCl-coupled CDs, a novel photocatalyst CDs-BiO1-xCl was prepared by modifying BiO1-xCl with CDs to construct a light-controlled round-the-clock CPPA system. To the best of our knowledge, it is found for the first time that CDs-BiO1-xCl in association with PS activation provides a promising synergistic degradation system of OPs via the visible-light-modulated radical/non-radical complementary degradation pathways that inhibits the interference of co-existing anions and NOMs to achieve the round-the-clock degradation of pollutants in water matrix.

Herein, a visible-light regulated around-the-clock CDs-BiO1-xCl/PS system with proper amounts of loaded CDs and appreciable Ov was prepared via a facile solvothermal-calcination for the efficient decomposition of OPs such as BPA, AAP, phenol, and RhB under dark or visible light irradiation. The glycerol was employed as a solvent to regulate the formation of Ov in BiOCl nanosheets as it reacts with the oxygen present on the (001) surfaces of BiOCl (Jiang et al., 2013). The micromorphology, crystal structure, optical properties, elemental compositions, electronic structures, and charge transfer mechanism of the synthesized CDs-BiO1-xCl nanosheets were investigated through advanced characterization techniques. In addition, theoretical calculations were conducted to further explore the influence of Ov on the electronic structure of BiOCl. The pollutant removal performance and mechanism of the CDs-BiO1-xCl/PS system under dark (CDs-BiO1-xCl/PS/Dark system) and visible light irradiation (CDs-BiO1-xCl/PS/Vis system) were systematically explored. The biotoxicity analysis were used to identify intermediate product toxicity. Moreover, the radical/non-radical processes of PS activation and the key active sites of the system were investigated systematically by electron paramagnetic resonance (EPR) spectroscopy, in-situ Raman analysis, probe experiments, in-situ infrared spectroscopy and density functional theory (DFT) calculations. In summary, this study aims to elucidate the switching mechanism of electron transfer pathways and radical generation during the optical "ON-OFF" on the heterostructure of CDs-BiO1-xCl, thus deepening the understanding of visible light-regulated round-the-clock pollutant degradation systems.

Citric acid (C6H8O7), bismuth (III) nitrate pentahydrate (Bi(NO3)3·5H2O), p-benzoquinone (C6H4O2, BQ), bisphenol A (C15H16O2, BPA), nitrobenzene (C6H5NO2, NB), benzoic acid (C7H6O2, BA) and phenol (C6H5OH) were procured from Aladdin (China). The details of the used chemicals are available in Supporting Information (Text S1).

In order to optimize the concentration of CDs, different mass percent ratios (1, 5, 10, and 15 %) of CDs to BiO1-xCl were prepared. For the preparation of 5 %CDs-BiO1-xCl, about 10 mg CDs powder was mixed in 8 mL ethanol following the addition of 190 mg BiO1-xCl under continuous stir condition. Meanwhile, the mixture was heated at 60°C for the complete evaporation of ethanol. Ultimately, the obtained powder was calcined at 400°C under N2 atmosphere with a heating rate 3 °C/min for 3 h. A similar procedure was used for the preparation of 1 %, 10 %, and 15 % CDs-BiO1-xCl composite samples by varying the concentration of CDs. For comparison, 5 %CDs-BiOCl sample was also fabricated using BiOCl instead of BiO1-xCl that was calcined in the air rather than in N2 atmosphere (Figure S1). The details of prepared CDs, BiOCl and BiO1-xCl are available in Supporting Information (Text S2).

The morphology, crystallinity, chemical, and optical structure of fabricated nanocomposites were characterized by scanning electron microscopy (SEM), transmission electron microscopy (TEM), high resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM), X-ray diffraction (XRD), EPR, Fourier-transformed infrared spectra (FT-IR), UV-vis diffuse reflection spectroscopy (UV-vis DRS), X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS), photoluminescence (PL), and time-resolved photoluminescence (time-resolved PL). The details of employed instruments are provided in Supporting Information (Text S3).

The photocatalytic performance of fabricated nanomaterials was tested by degrading the model pollutant BPA (a typical environmental endocrine disruptor) in the presence of PS under visible light irradiation. A 250 mL double-glass container with a water recirculation system for reactor cooling was used. A 300W Xe-lamp having a light intensity 2100 W/m2 with a cut-off filter 420 nm was used as a source of light (Fig. S2). In order to achieve the absorption-desorption equilibrium, photocatalyst, and BPA suspension was stirred under dark condition for 30 min. A specific amount of sample was removed at a certain time interval following the filtration through 0.22 µm filter. The experimental detail can be found in Supporting Information (Text S4).

The morphology and phase structure of photocatalysts were observed by SEM, HRTEM and XRD as shown in Fig. 1 . The BiOCl exhibited a large number of constructed sheets with a smooth surface (Fig. 1a), whereas the BiO1-xCl catalyst was composed of ultra-thin sheets gathered in a typical flower-like shape (Fig. 1b). In addition, HRTEM and XRD data suggest that the modification with CDs and Ov does not change the crystal phase of BiOCl (Text S5). The layer distance of the (001) crystal plane is measured to be approximately 0.736 nm (Fig. 1c and d), which is consistent with the referenced report (Cui et al. 2018). The 5 %CDs-BiO1-xCl nanosheets showed high crystallinity (Fig. 1e). The diffraction peaks of four different as-synthesized samples were well indexed with the typical peaks of the standard BiOCl phase (JPCDS No.06-249) (Fig. 1). In conclusion, HRTEM and XRD data suggest that the crystal phase of BiOCl remains unchanged after modification with CDs and Ov.

In order to further confirm the coexistence of Ov and CDs in BiO1-xCl-based photocatalysts, FT-IR, XPS, and EPR analyses were carried out. FT-IR spectra of CDs show (Fig. 1g) peaks at 1671, 1576, 1401, and 1187 cm-1 corresponding well to the vibrational stretching of COOH, C=C, C-O, and C-O-C bonds, respectively (Xu et al., 2019a). Moreover, all BiOCl-based photocatalysts exhibit obvious peaks at 520-530 cm-1 and 1050 cm-1, which might be due to the stretching vibration of Bi-O and Bi-Cl bonds, respectively (Gao et al., 2019). Hence, 5 %CDs-BiO1-xCl possesses the characteristic peaks of CDs and BiO1-xCl, which shows the successful combination of CDs and BiO1-xCl (Fig. 1g). According to the results of the XPS spectrum, comparing with BiOCl, BiO1-xCl shows two additional peaks at 163.07 eV and 157.56 eV, which is attributed to the occurrence of Bi(+3-x) resulting from the formation of Ov (Ye et al., 2015). Meanwhile, in the 5 %CDs-BiO1-xCl composite, a shift of the Bi(+3-x) peaks towards higher binding energies (164.2 eV and 158.85 eV) is attributed to the transfer of electrons from BiO1-xCl to the CDs during the formation of the composite, which results in higher electron density on CDs favoring the subsequent activation of PS at CDs surface. The peaks at 199.8 eV (Cl 2p1/2), and 198.19 eV (Cl 2p3/2) were also observed in BiO1-xCl and 5 %CDs-BiO1-xCl samples which is consistent with BiOCl sample. Three notable peaks of C1s were observed at 284.56 eV (C-C/C=C bond), 285.47 eV (C-O bond), and 287.86 eV (O=C-OH bond) for 5 %CDs-BiO1-xCl, signifying the successful bonding of CDs and BiO1-xCl across the heterojunction interface (Liqun Ye, 2015) (Text S5). Moreover, EPR was used to verify the formation of Ov defects in the catalyst lattice as shown in Fig. 1l. BiO1-xCl and 5 %CDs-BiO1-xCl samples presented obvious characteristic spectral peak with a g-value of 2.001, caused by the unpaired electrons captured by Ov while this peak was not detected in BiOCl and 5 %CDs-BiOCl samples (Wang et al., 2023a). Notably, the peak intensity of 5 %CDs-BiO1-xCl was stronger as compared to BiO1-xCl, indicating that the concentration of Ov was improved after calcination in inert atmosphere. All these results manifest that CDs and Ov coexist in the 5 %CDs-BiO1-xCl nano-catalyst.

The optoelectronic features of the photocatalysts were determined by UV-vis DRS, PL, VB-XPS spectra, and DFT calculation. A notable change in the optical properties of 5 %CDs-BiO1-xCl was detected due to the introduction of Ov and CDs. The absorption band edges of BiOCl, BiO1-xCl, and 5 %CDs-BiO1-xCl were located at 355 nm, 371 nm, and 426 nm, respectively (Fig. 2 a). All BiO1-xCl-based composites modified with different quantities of CDs possessed significantly broader absorption edges as compared to pristine BiOCl. Thus, the existence of CDs and Ov optimized the electronic structure which narrows the band gap width of pure BiOCl leading to the enhanced absorbance of visible light for boosted photocatalysis.

To highlight the effect of Ov on enhancing light absorption and band gap reduction, the optical structure of BiO1-xCl was calculated by DFT. The initial position of VB and CB showed no change after Ov modification (Fig. 2e and f). Nevertheless, a mid-gap state was observed in the band gap of BiO1-xCl, which was calculated as the energy level position of Ov at 1.31 eV formed due to the Bi 6p and O 2p orbitals. The induced Ov helps to trap the excited charges and also boost the charge separation along with the extension in visible light absorption for accelerated photocatalysis.

PL spectroscopy and electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) were conducted to study the transfer and separation effects of e--h+ pairs. Fig. 2b displays the PL emission spectra of the as-prepared photocatalysts. The emission peak intensity of BiO1-xCl was reduced significantly due to the introduction of Ov into BiOCl lattice compared to pristine BiOCl. This modification improves the separation efficiency of photoinduced e--h+ pairs and also enhances the internal electric field strength of the Ov-modified BiO1-xCl. Remarkably, the PL intensities of all CDs loaded BiO1-xCl-based samples were dramatically reduced. These findings represent that the incorporation of highly conductive CDs is beneficial for the suppression of charge carrier recombination. Therefore, the modification of BiOCl with CDs and Ov can remarkably improve the charge separation efficiency during photocatalysis. Moreover, the time-resolved PL spectra were conducted to further determine the dynamics of electron migration at the heterojunction interface. The PL decay constants (τA) of 5 %CDs-BiO1-xCl was 1.822 ns which is relatively lower than BiOCl (28.844 ns) and BiO1-xCl (2.3681 ns) (Fig. 2c and Table S1). The electron transfer ability of different prepared materials (BiOCl, BiO1-xCl, and 5 %CDs-BiO1-xCl) was examined through electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS). As shown in Figure S15, the 5 %CDs-BiO1-xCl exhibits the smallest arc radius in the EIS Nyquist plot compared to other materials, reflecting the lowest charge transfer resistance (Rct) in 5 %CDs-BiO1-xCl (Zhang et al., 2023). While the BiO1-xCl has a smaller arc radius compared to pure BiOCl, suggesting that the introduction of Ov enhances electron transport. In addition, the incorporation of CDs significantly reduced the Rct due to their excellent electron transfer capabilities, thereby enhancing charge transfer efficiency. In conclusion, the 5 %CDs-BiO1-xCl has superior electron transfer ability compared with BiOCl and BiO1-xCl. These findings confirms that the modification of both CDs and Ov can effectively promote the electron transfer across the heterojunction interface in 5 %CDs-BiO1-xCl.

The electronic structure of BiOCl and 5 %CDs-BiO1-xCl were investigated through Mott-Schottky analysis, UV vis DRS and XPS. Mott-Schottky analysis for the 5 %CDs-BiO1-xCl and the BiOCl were obtained (Figure S16). The calculated flat band (Efb) potential of the 5 %CDs-BiO1-xCl and BiOCl were found to be -0.47 V and -0.75 V (vs. Ag/AgCl), respectively. The Efb values were subsequently converted versus normal hydrogen electrode (NHE) ((Efb (NHE, pH = 7) = Efb (Ag/AgCl) + 0.198 - 0.059*(7 - pH of the electrolyte)) (Das et al., 2022). Hence, The Fermi level (Ef) of the 5 %CDs-BiO1-xCl and BiOCl (approximate to the Efb of the photocatalysts) were estimated to be -0.27 and -0.55 V (vs. NHE), respectively. The VB-XPS indicated that the edges of the valence band (VB) for the 5 %CDs-BiO1-xCl and BiOCl were measured to be 2.28 and 2.61 eV with respect to the Ef (Fig. 2d), respectively. Hence, the calculated VB potential of 5 %CDs-BiO1-xCl was 2.0 V (vs. NHE) and that of BiOCl was 2.1 V (vs. NHE). In term of the equation Eg = 1240/λg (Eg is the band gap and λg is the absorption edge of the catalyst), the calculated band gap energies of 5 %CDs-BiO1-xCl and BiOCl were 2.91 and 3.49 eV, respectively (Fig. 2a). Correspondingly, the conduction band edge (CB) of 5 %CDs-BiO1-xCl was -0.90 V (vs. NHE) which was more positive than BiOCl (-1.4 V vs. NHE). The 5 %CDs-BiO1-xCl had a lower band gap energy which was attributed to its lower CB caused by the impurity level due to the introduction of Ov as compared to BiOCl.

The efficient round-the-clock degradation performance of the 5 %CDs-BiO1-xCl/PS system for the persistent organic pollutants was investigated under visible light irradiation or dark conditions. Insignificant degradation of BPA was noticed in control experiments in the presence of visible light or PS (Fig. 3 a). In contrast, the BPA degradation efficiency was remarkably improved in the presence of photocatalyst. BiO1-xCl/PS/Vis system showed 34 % degradation efficiency as compared to BiOCl/PS/Vis system (10 %) after 15 min. Moreover, the effect of different concentrations of CDs in BiO1-xCl for BPA degradation in the presence of PS is presented in Fig. 3b. Notably, the removal rate of micro-pollutants was increased to some extent with the increase in CDs concentration and then decreased after a certain limit of CDs concentration under visible light. Interestingly, the BPA was completely removed (100 %) with 5 % CDs dosage (5 %CDs-BiO1-xCl) which is attributed to the facilitating electron transfer characteristics of CDs (Xu et al., 2019a). Although the photocatalytic decomposition of BPA was significant with the CDs dosage up to 5 % (Fig. 3b) the photodegradation rate constants reduced from 0.393 to 0.234 min-1 when the proportion of CDs rose from 5 % to 15 % (Figure S17). The decrease in the rate constant at high concentrations of CDs was attributed to the competitive visible-light absorption of CDs with respect to BiO1-xCl. Among all samples, BPA was completely decomposed by 5 %CDs-BiO1-xCl/PS/Vis system within 15 min in the presence of 2 mM PS, while the total organic carbon (TOC) removal efficiency was 67.4 % (Text S6). The quantum yield (QY) of different composites were calculated for evaluating photocatalytic performance (Ahmadi and Kim, 2024). As shown in the Table S2, the composites provided a set of QY values ranging from 8.3E-09 to 6.9E-08. Accordingly, 5 %CDs-BiO1-xCl demonstrated the highest QY (6.9E-08), followed by BiO1-xCl (1.6E-08) and BiOCl (8.3E-09), indicating that absorbed photons were most efficiently converted into photochemical reactions for the pollutant degradation in 5 %CDs-BiO1-xCl/PS/Vis system. The above results were in agreement with the experimental results of UV-vis DRS and PL, i.e., the modification of CDs and Ov significantly broadened the light absorption range and improved the photogenerated carrier separation efficiency of the composites (Fig. 2), which therefore indicated that the 5 %CDs-BiO1-xCl could capture and convert photons in a more efficient manner to produce significant amounts of ROSs for BPA degradation. Surprisingly, 5 %CDs-BiO1-xCl/PS system also showed a complete removal of BPA under dark condition (without light) (Fig. 3a). Although the BPA degradation rate through 5 %CDs-BiO1-xCl/PS/Dark system was slower as compared to 5 %CDs-BiO1-xCl/PS/Vis system, this result suggested that 5 %CDs-BiO1-xCl can effectively activate PS in both dark and light conditions. In summary, the 5 %CDs-BiO1-xCl/PS system can effectively degrade the contaminants in the round-the-clock manner despite the absence of light.

In addition, the performance of the proposed system was also tested for para-acetaminophen (AAP), phenol, and rhodamine B (RhB). All these target pollutants were fully degraded within 30 min while the removal efficiency of phenol was 54.4 % in 60 min as illustrated in Fig. 3c. Basically, ·OH and ·SO4 - have strong electrophilicity and reaction capability with unsaturated organic compounds containing aromatic rings (Text S7) (He et al., 2022). The aromatic ring with -OH group in AAP and BPA makes them more available for the attack of free radicals which laterally leads toward ring opening. Compared with other compounds, the chemical structure of phenol is comparatively stable containing a delocalized
π
7
8
bond, leads to its low degradability (Lei Yang et al., 2019). From the obtained degradation activities, it is concluded that 5 %CDs-BiO1-xCl/PS/Vis system showed excellent non-selective oxidation performance and practical application potential for rapidly decomposing refractory organic pollutants. The reusability test of 5 %CDs-BiO1-xCl was further conducted to test its stability for pollutant removal after five consecutive cycles. The experimental and characterization findings showed the high stability of the prepared material (Fig. 3d) and a no significant change in crystallinity (Figure S11) and morphology (Figure S12) was observed (Text S8). In order to further investigate the practical application potential of 5 %CDs-BiO1-xCl/PS/Vis system, the effects of coexisting ions were studies in detail, as these ions significantly influence the performance of an employed system. NO3 - and SO4 2- showed a slight inhibitory effect on BPA removal (Fig. 3e) due to the scavenging effect of ·SO4 - (Bacha et al., 2020). In addition, Cl- and CO3 2- showed an adverse effect for BPA degradation while Cl– reacted with ROSs leading to the production of reactive species including ClHO, ·Cl, and ·Cl2 -. Besides, the hydrolyzation of CO3 2- generates OH- ions and HCO3 - ions (causing an increase in pH that generally affects photocatalytic activity). The Humic acid (HA) at various concentrations (5-15 mg/L) showed little impact on the degradation rate of BPA (Fig. 3e and Text S9) on 5 %CDs-BiO1-xCl/PS/Vis system, indicating that the system exhibited strong resistance to interference from HA. To better estimate the applicability of the CDs-BiO1-xCl/PS/Vis synergistic system for the degradation of contaminants in natural environments, the removal of BPA in different water matrices was investigated, including secondary effluent, tap and river water, the chemical composition (detected ions) of which is presented in Table S3. A complete removal of BPA was observed in all real water samples but the degradation rate was higher in DI water (0.393 min-1) under the same reaction condition as depicted in Fig. 3f. In comparison, the reaction rates in secondary effluent, tap water, and river water were 0.290, 0.351, and 0.282 min-1, respectively (Figure S18), which was slightly lower than that in DI water due to the inappreciable effect of coexisting ions (Zheng, 2023). Overall, 5 %CDs-BiO1-xCl/PS/Vis system showed excellent stability and potential application for pollutants degradation in the actual water matrices.

In order to gain a more comprehensive understanding of the degradation pathways of BPA and to identify the toxicity of its intermediate products, the environmental friendliness and application potential of the light-controlled synergistic system were revealed. In this regard, the reaction intermediates during BPA degradation were monitored through LCMS-IT-TOF and a degradation pathway is proposed in detail (Text S10 and Figure S14). As shown in Table S4, four main intermediates were identified such as 4-(2-(4-hydroxyphenyl) propan-2-yl) cyclohexa-3,5-diene-1,2-dione (product A, m/z = 241.08), 4-isopropenecatechol (product B, m/z = 149.99), 1-(2,4-dihydroxyphenyl) ethanone (product C, m/z = 151.08) and maleic acid (product D, m/z = 112.99). The continuous attack of ROSs further oxidizes the intermediates, ultimately leading toward ring opening and short chain molecule formation. According to the TOC data, the mineralization rate of BPA degradation was 67.4 %. Therefore, the biotoxicity of solution after reaction was studied by considering the effect of intermediates on the growth of bacterial species E. coli and B. subtilis. Generally, the biotoxicity analysis showed whether the treated solution contains any harmful byproducts. If there is no effect on microbial species then it indicates that employed system is highly effective in degrading the target pollutant without producing the toxic byproducts. The experimental findings suggest that colony densities of both species did not show any appreciable change in treated solution (Fig. 3g and h) as compared to the blank sample and original BPA solution (Fig. S19). Similarly, the growth curves of the two bacteria mentioned above (Figure S20) were hardly affected indicating that the 5 %CDs-BiO1-xCl/PS/Vis system did not cause secondary pollution, which is highly environmentally friendly.

ce:section.ce:section-title: In-depth mechanism of 5 %CDs-BiO1-xCl/PS systems under dark or light
ce:section.ce:para: To fully understand the mechanism of the high-efficiency around-the-clock system, EPR studies, quenching experiments, electrochemical analysis, in-situ Raman and FT-IR analyses, probe experiments, and DFT calculations were conducted. Combining experimental and computational results, there are two potential pathways for the activation of PS in the CPPA systems. The first pathway involves direct chemical activation of PS, resulting in the formation of complexes at the C site of 5 %CDs-BiO1-xCl under dark conditions, which facilitates the degradation of pollutants through a non-radical electron transfer mechanism. In contrast, the second pathway occurs through the photoactivation of PS, primarily utilizing a radical mechanism. In other words, it is promising to regulate the radical and non-radical activation pathways of PS with 5 %CDs-BiO1-xCl catalyst through controlling "ON-OFF" of light. This visible-light-controlled system has broad prospects for round-the-clock degradation of pollutants while effectively mitigating the interference from co-existing anions and NOMs present in real water reactions.

In the 5 %CDs-BiO1-xCl/PS/Dark system, to visualize the presence of radicals (·OH, ·SO4 -, and ·O2 -) and non-radicals (1O2, h+ and complexes), EPR spectroscopy and quenching experiments were performed (Fig. 4 ). As shown in Fig. 4a, negligible signals (DMPO-·OH- and DMPO-·SO4 -) were found from the EPR spectra under dark conditions, indicating that minimal amounts of ·OH and ·SO4 - were hardly produced. In addition, the presence of ·O2 - and 1O2 was detected using DMPO (Fig. 4b) and TEMP (Fig. 4c) traps. Only inappreciable signals for DMPO-·O2 - and TEMP-1O2 were detected, indicating that the system barely produced a tiny amount of ROSs. Despite the absence of EPR signals, its ability to efficiently degrade BPA under dark conditions suggests that the degradation of pollutants may occurs via an electron transfer pathway. In order to elucidate the role of each ROSs in the degradation process, chemical quenching experiments were carried out under dark condition. Ethanol traps both ·OH and ·SO4 -, and tert-butyl-alcohol (TBA) scavenges ·OH- (Chen et al., 2021). The p-benzoquinone (BQ) and ethylenediaminetetraacetic acid disodium (EDTA-2Na) quench ·O2 and h+ , respectively. Furfuryl alcohol (FFA) was used to quench 1O2 (Du et al., 2020). It can be seen in Fig. 4d that the 5 %CDs-BiO1-xCl/PS system is highly effective for the degradation of BPA even under dark condition in the presence of various radical scavengers (ethanol, TBA, BQ, and FFA). The BPA degradation primarily occurred through electron transfer pathways (complexes) rather than through radical mechanisms under dark condition. These quenching results are in good agreement with EPR findings (Fig. 4a-c) as no signal of ROSs was noticed under dark condition.

FT-IR analyses, In-situ Raman and electrochemical analysis were conducted to further validate the non-radical PS activation mechanisms and to identify the sites where the PS forms complexes with the 5 %CDs-BiO1-xCl. As shown in Fig. 4e, the O-O characteristic peaks at 1094 cm-1 of PS diminished over time in the FT-IR spectra (Yang et al., 2020), indicating that PS reacted with 5 %CDs-BiO1-xCl in dark. Furthermore, PS was mixed with 5 %CDs-BiO1-xCl for 10 minutes, then the mixture was pump-filtered, and the PS content in the filtrate was measured. The slight decrease in PS concentration following filtration was attributed to the formation of complexes between PS and 5 %CDs-BiO1-xCl (Figure S21). In addition, a distinct complex signal was detected in the Raman spectra arriving from the binding interaction between PS and 5 %CDs-BiO1-xCl surface (Fig. 4f). Moreover, a similar complex signal was detected with only CDs and PS as well (Figures S22), suggesting that PS is bound to the CDs surface rather than the BiO1-xCl surface. The electrochemically active surface area (ECSA) serves as a crucial metric for quantifying the active sites on the catalyst surface. Hence, cyclic voltammetry (CV) was performed at different scan rates, and the corresponding double-layer capacitance (Cdl) values were calculated, as shown in Fig. 5 . The Cdl values of the catalysts range from 6.8 × 10⁻5 to 9.2 × 10⁻5 mF/cm2. Notably, after modification with CDs, both 5 %CDs-BiO1-xCl and 5 %CDs-BiOCl exhibit significantly higher Cdl values as compared to BiO1-xCl and BiOCl. Furthermore, the calculated ECSA values for 5 %CDs-BiO1-xCl, 5 %CDs-BiOCl, BiO1-xCl, and BiOCl are 15.3 × 10⁻5, 13.5 × 10⁻5, 11.6 × 10⁻5 and 11.3 × 10⁻5 cm2, respectively. The ECSA values of BiO1-xCl and BiOCl exhibit a similarity, whereas 5 %CDs-BiO1-xCl exhibits the highest ECSA value. These results suggest that the introduction of CDs effectively enhances the number of electrochemically active sites, thereby improving the catalytic performance.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Radical mechanism upon visible light irradiation
ce:section.ce:para: The primary ROSs in the visible-light-driven CPPA system, along with the transformation of complexes formed at the C site under visible light irradiation, were elucidated using EPR spin trapping and quenching experiments. According to the EPR spectra (Fig. 4), the strong signals of DMPO-·OH and DMPO-·SO4 - adducts appeared under visible-light irradiation, which proved that both ·OH and ·SO4 - were produced in the 5 %CDs-BiO1-xCl/PS/Vis system (Fig. 4a). Significantly, the typical peaks with an intensity ratio of 1:1:1:1 of DMPO-·O2 - were observed in methanol solution after irradiation verifying the generation ·O2 - under visible light (Xu et al., 2019b). In addition, the presence of 1O2 and h+ was confirmed using TEMP (Fig. 4c) and TEMPO (Fig. 4g) traps in the photocatalytic system. In conclusion, 4 types of ROSs (·SO4 -, ·OH, ·O2 - and 1O2) and h+ were produced in the photocatalytic system. Chemical quenching experiments were performed to investigate the respective contribution of each ROS for BPA photocatalytic degradation. In contrast to the experimental results in dark, ethanol and TBA showed significantly inhibitory effects on the photocatalytic reaction (Fig. 4h). In the 5 %CDs-BiO1-xCl/PS/Vis system, the photodegradation rate constant for blank sample, ethanol, and TBA were 0.39 min-1, 0.09 min-1 and 0.17 min-1, respectively. These results suggested that ·OH-, and ·SO4 - were produced in the visible-light-driven system which promoted the BPA degradation. In addition, EDTA-2Na showed a high inhibition effect on the BPA degradation (0.028 min-1), followed by BQ (0.006 min-1), while the inhibiting effect of FFA (0.072 min-1) was relatively low. Thus, all generated ROSs including ·OH, ·SO4 -, h+ , 1O2, ·O2 - played their roles during the photocatalytic removal of BPA, while h+ , ·SO4 - and ·O2 - were identified the dominant ones.

To further investigate the significant role of the complexes in photocatalytic processes, EPR spectroscopy, photocurrent measurements and probe experiments were conducted. Potassium dichromate (K2Cr2O7), a common electron scavenger, was employed to inhibit formation of ROSs by quenching possible electron transfer in solution (Duan et al., 2021). In comparison to the 5 %CDs-BiO1-xCl/PS/Vis/K2Cr2O7 system, the 5 %CDs-BiO1-xCl/PS/Vis system displayed more pronounced DMPO-·OH- and DMPO-·SO4 - signals, which further suggested that the radicals were mainly generated by the complexes under light (Fig. 4a). In addition, the 5 %CDs-BiO1-xCl/PS/Vis system exhibited the highest photocurrent among all visible-light systems (Figure S23). This enhancement can be attributed to that complexes facilitated the charge transfer of photogenerated carriers subsequently enhancing the generation of ROSs (Liu et al., 2020). Furthermore, probe experiments and EPR spectroscopy indicated that the surface complexes formed through the non-radical pathway can further produce ·SO4 - under light. According to the reported rate constants of radicals (Table S5) and the results of probe experiments, the steady-state concentrations were calculated as 9 × 10-11 M for ·OH and 1 × 10-9 M for ·SO4 - in 5 %CDs-BiO1-xCl/PS/Vis system, which were significantly higher than those in the 5 %CDs-BiO1-xCl/PS/Dark system (3 × 10-11 M for ·SO4 - and 2.9 × 10-12 M for ·OH) (Fig. 4i) (Dou et al., 2022). EPR spectroscopy reveals that the signals of DMPO-·SO4⁻, DMPO-·OH, and DMPO-·O2⁻ are significantly stronger in 5 %CDs-BiO1-xCl/PS/Vis system compared to 5 %CDs-BiOCl/PS/Vis system, indicating that Ov enhances the generation of multiple ROSs (Figure S24a and b). TEMP-1O2 measurements further confirm that 5 %CDs-BiO1-xCl produces more TEMP-1O2 signals (Figure S24c), suggesting an increased capacity for 1O2 generation. In contrast, when TEMPO was used as a trapping agent, the EPR signal of 5 %CDs-BiO1-xCl under visible-light irradiation was significantly weaker than that of 5 %CDs-BiOCl, suggesting that the presence of oxygen vacancies in 5 %CDs-BiO1-xCl facilitates the generation of a large number of holes under visible-light illumination (Figure S24d). This is consistent with the previously mentioned findings (Fig. 2) from the electronic structure analysis (UV-vis DRS, EIS, PL, and time-resolved PL). Ov in BiOCl enhances visible-light absorption and suppresses charge carrier recombination, facilitating the transformation of surface complexes into various ROSs under illumination. In summary, the EPR and DFT results demonstrate that Ov accelerates the transformation of reactive complexes on CDs into ·SO4⁻.

Additionally, the conversion of ·SO4 - during the photocatalytic reaction was further investigated by employing the NB, nitrotetrazolium blue chloride (NBT) and 1,3-diphenylisobenzofuran (DPBF) as molecular probes for ·OH, ·O2 - and 1O2, respectively. In the NB/5 %CDs-BiO1-xCl/PS suspension, the degradation of NB was inhibited with TBA as compared to the blank experiment (NB/5 %CDs-BiO1-xCl/PS without TBA), suggesting the generation of ·OH. However, a significant inhibition of NB was observed with EtOH which highlights the quenching of both ·OH and ·SO4 - radicals as shown in Figure S25. These findings support our hypothesis that ·SO4 - was generated in the CPPA system which can further produce ·OH (Eq. 6). Furthermore, NBT and DPBF were used as a specific probe for superoxide and singlet oxygen radicals (a primary reactive oxygen species in the photocatalytic system (Eqs. 7-9)) from ·SO4 - and ·OH- (Liu et al., 2022, Wan et al., 2022, Wang et al., 2023b). A dominant signal of NBT was observed with 500 mM ethanol (EtOH) which signifies that ·SO4 - and ·OH- were converted into ·O2 - after 1 min reaction. Likewise, the DPBF also showed the formation of singlet oxygen under different reaction systems (Eq. 5) and Eqs. (7-9) (Liu et al., 2022, Wan et al., 2022, Wang et al., 2023b). The weak signal of DPBF with EtOH might be due to the quenching of ·SO4 - and ·OH- which may inhibit the conversion of these radicals into 1O2 (Figure S26). Thus, the above results reveal that the edge carbon atoms (C) of CDs in the photocatalyst can provide active sites for reacting with PS to form complexes via a non-radical pathway. In addition, Ov in the photocatalyst enhance visible-light absorption, suppress charge carrier recombination, and facilitate the conversion of complexes into ·SO4⁻ and other ROSs when the entire reaction system is exposed to visible-light irradiation.
(1) 5 %CDs-BiO1-xCl + PS → Complexes
(2) 5 %CDs- BiO1-xCl + hv → h+ + e-
(3) Complexes + e- →·SO4 - + SO4 2-
(4)
e- + O2 → ·O2 -
(5) ·O2 - + ·O2 - → 21O2
(6) ·SO4 -+ H2O → ·OH + SO4 2-
(7) 2H2O + 2·O2 - → 1O2 + 2OH- + H2O2
(8) ·OH + H2O2 → ·H2O + H2O
(9) 2·H2O → ·O2 - + 4H+
(10) Complexes, ROSs (·O2 -/SO4 -/1O2/·OH) and h+ + BPA → CO2 + H2O

In summary, the 5 %CDs-BiO1-xCl were successfully prepared for efficient round-the-clock degradation of organic pollutants in water, which achieved visible light regulated radical/non-radical PS activation. The experimental and DFT results showed that CDs can effectively bind with PS, forming oxidizing complexes. In the absence of light, the complexes efficiently degrade the pollutant by the electron-transferring non-radical pathway (5 %CDs-BiO1-xCl/PS/Dark system). In addition, the degradation mechanism of pollutants by radical pathway in the 5 %CDs-BiO1-xCl/PS/Vis system under light regulation had been revealed. The synergistic contribution of the electron transport of CDs and the introduction of Ov significantly boosted the absorbance ability in the visible light region and suppressed the recombination of photo-generated electron-hole pairs. As a result, more excited electrons participate in the transformation of complexes for the generation of reactive species to boost pollutant degradation. The main oxidizing species during the removal of BPA were h+ , ·O2 - and ·SO4 - as confirmed through radical quenching experiments and EPR measurement. In the 5 %CDs-BiO1-xCl/PS/Vis system, BPA was eliminated within 15 min and mineralization efficiency reached 67.4 % under the optimum reaction condition. In addition, the stability and recyclability of 5 %CDs-BiO1-xCl were outstanding. Furthermore, the optimized sample also showed excellent degradation activities towards other multiple organic contaminants (AAP, phenol, and RhB). Consequently, the round-the-clock 5 %CDs-BiO1-xCl/PS system offers a light-controlled pathway for pollutant degradation, presenting a wide range of applications in water pollution treatment.
Any additional relevant notes should be placed here.

Aziz Ur Rahim Bacha: Writing – review & editing, Investigation. Wenbiao Jin: Writing – review & editing, Validation. Lei Yang: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Funding acquisition, Conceptualization.
